coaching.subject: retry and LockTimeout failure mode coverage

coaching.coaching: Pin down rollback procedure acceptance criteria with a concrete worked example.
coaching.subject: rollback procedure acceptance criteria

coaching.coaching: Test the riskiest assumption behind backward-compaction safety of plan_review_phase first.
coaching.subject: backward-compaction safety of plan_review_phase

coaching.coaching: Record an ADR for sibling file-conflict ordering edge policy — it reads like a one-way door.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

evidence: tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_plan_review_gate.py: 2bbd-87f6-8765-4b00 & 7c27-10fc-4fe6-4b56 (no ordering edge)
tests/unit/test_plan_review_prerequisites.py: 7c27-10fc-4fe6-4b56 & 9417-a700-297d-4701 (no ordering edge)
findings.finding: Sibling tickets touch the same file(s) with no ordering edge.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a depends_on/blocks edge to serialize, or partition the file ownership.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC covering pre-sign lock failure dispositions: retry exhaustion after three attempts and LockTimeout both return a retryable unsigned review rather than a signed one.
evidence: 'On revision change retry outside-lock collection at most three times; exhaustion returns retryable unsigned'
'LockTimeout during the pre-sign commit has the same retryable unsigned disposition'
No AC references retryable-unsigned, retry exhaustion, or lock-timeout disposition
findings.finding: The plan describes explicit pre-sign lock failure modes (retry exhaustion after three outside-lock attempts returns retryable unsigned; LockTimeout during pre-sign commit has the same retryable unsigned disposition) but no AC verifies either failure path, leaving these specified behaviors unverifiable via the acceptance criteria.
findings.location: Settled design > Signed plan-material pins (locking/retry protocol); Acceptance Criteria (no corresponding AC)
scenarios: A high-contention tracker with frequent concurrent writes causes retry exhaustion; the unsigned disposition is never verified.
A slow commit on the pre-sign lock triggers LockTimeout; the retryable-unsigned path is tested but never gated by an AC.
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'Pre-sign retry exhaustion (three outside-lock attempts) and LockTimeout during the pre-sign commit each return a retryable unsigned review.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC verifying that both documented rollback paths leave existing attestations intact and require no stored-attestation rewrite.
evidence: 'To disable only the opt-in gates, set enforce_plan_material_pins=false and require_plan_review_for_close=false; this does not change the fixed execution-review floor.'
'To remove the 0.80 execution-review behavior, first disable both gates, then deploy the preceding code version; a runtime floor flag is intentionally not added.'
'No stored attestation must be rewritten.'
No AC references rollback, flag-disablement procedure, or attestation-rewrite constraint
findings.finding: The plan explicitly describes two rollback paths with specific operational steps (disable-flags path and remove-0.80-floor code path) as named deliverables, but no AC verifies that either rollback procedure leaves stored attestations intact and services operational.
findings.location: Settled design > Enforcement and rollout (Rollback section); Acceptance Criteria (no corresponding AC)
scenarios: A rollback to the preceding code version corrupts plan_review_phase in compacted snapshots; no AC catches this.
An implementation that requires attestation rewriting during rollback passes all ACs.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the packed-evaluation fidelity measurement protocol: specify the fixture corpus (e.g., the existing tail-item fixture set), the TP/FP/FN definitions for recall and false-acceptance over prerequisite coverage records, and whether 'no lower / no higher' means exact equality or a stated numeric tolerance (e.g., ±0).
evidence: 'packed recall must be no lower, packed false acceptance no higher, every prerequisite must receive a record, and attribution errors must be zero' — metric terms left undefined
'release-gated against a one-prerequisite-per-call gold baseline' — baseline corpus not identified
No section defines TP/FP/FN over what population, nor any tolerance for 'no lower'
findings.finding: The packed-prerequisite fidelity gate (AC: 'Packed prerequisite evaluation matches the one-per-call fidelity contract') names four properties — packed recall no lower, packed false acceptance no higher, every prerequisite receives a record, attribution errors zero — but does not specify the measurement protocol: what test corpus or fixture set is used, how recall and false acceptance are computed (TP/FP/FN definitions over which population), and what numerical tolerance ('no lower') means in practice (exact equality, epsilon, statistical). An executing agent cannot write this gate test without inventing these definitions.
findings.location: Settled design / Prerequisite consistency review — 'Packed behavior uses the existing whole-child bin-packing pattern and is release-gated…'; AC line: 'Packed prerequisite evaluation matches the one-per-call fidelity contract.'
scenarios: Agent implementing the gate cannot determine what fixtures to run, how to score recall/false-acceptance, or when the gate passes vs fails — must invent the protocol, risking an incorrect or untestable gate.
Two separate agents implementing this independently would produce incompatible gate tests.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a subsection such as: 'Fidelity is measured over the mandatory tail-item fixture set. Recall = (prerequisites receiving consistent-or-finding record in packed run) / (same in one-per-call run); false acceptance = (prerequisites receiving consistent in packed run when one-per-call emits finding) / total. Gate requires recall == 1.0 and false_acceptance == 0.0 over the fixture set.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the fallback disjunction: state whether failure reverts to 'smaller-bin split' (recursive halving) or 'one-per-call' (each prerequisite in its own call), and specify any condition under which the other path is used.
evidence: 'Failure keeps the smaller-bin or one-per-call fallback' — 'or' is unresolved
No subsequent sentence resolves which fallback applies and under what condition
findings.finding: The fallback on packed-evaluation failure is stated as 'keeps the smaller-bin or one-per-call fallback' — a disjunction that leaves the executing agent to choose between two different strategies. This is a real design decision deferred to the executor.
findings.location: Settled design / Prerequisite consistency review — 'Failure keeps the smaller-bin or one-per-call fallback rather than shipping degraded packing.'
scenarios: Agent implementing the fallback picks one strategy; a later reviewer or a different agent picks the other, producing inconsistent behavior across implementations.
If the two strategies diverge on edge cases (e.g., a singleton bin), the unresolved choice produces different observable results.
findings.suggested_fix: For example: 'Failure reverts to one-per-call (each prerequisite in an isolated call); smaller-bin splitting is used only during the recursive context-limit retry path, not as a general fallback.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the retry budget (count and/or backoff) for LockTimeout at the pre-sign commit, or explicitly delegate to the existing system-wide lock-retry convention by name.
evidence: 'at most three times' applies only to revision-change retries
'LockTimeout during the pre-sign commit has the same retryable unsigned disposition' — disposition named but retry count for the caller not specified
No section states a max retry count or backoff for LockTimeout at the outer call site
findings.finding: The plan specifies that revision-change retries outside the write lock are bounded at 'at most three times', but does not state a bound or disposition for LockTimeout retries at the outer acquisition level. The LockTimeout disposition is given as 'retryable unsigned' but no retry count or backoff is specified, leaving the executor to choose.
findings.location: Settled design / Signed plan-material pins — 'On revision change retry outside-lock collection at most three times; exhaustion returns retryable unsigned. LockTimeout during the pre-sign commit has the same retryable unsigned disposition.'
scenarios: An agent implementing the retry loop must invent a retry budget for LockTimeout, potentially diverging from the existing lock-retry convention used elsewhere in the system.
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'LockTimeout retries follow the existing global write-lock retry policy (e.g., up to N attempts with exponential backoff); exhaustion returns retryable unsigned to the caller.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC verifying that a prerequisite singleton whose material exceeds the largest model window produces an unsigned too-large remediation rather than INDETERMINATE or a signed record.
evidence: 'A singleton escalates through models_at_or_above(); if it still exceeds the largest window, emit too-large remediation'
'Oversized input likewise yields unsigned too-large remediation' (Signed plan-material pins section)
No AC references too-large remediation or oversized-prerequisite handling
findings.finding: The plan specifies a too-large remediation path for oversized prerequisite singletons (escalate through models_at_or_above(); if largest window still exceeded, emit too-large remediation) but no AC verifies this behavior, leaving the entire singleton-oversized code path uncovered.
findings.location: Settled design > Prerequisite consistency review (bin-packing and model ladder); Acceptance Criteria (no corresponding AC)
scenarios: A prerequisite with extremely large material exceeds all model windows; the unsigned too-large path is implemented but never acceptance-tested.
An implementation that incorrectly emits INDETERMINATE instead of too-large remediation for oversized singletons passes all ACs.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen AC 21 (or add a companion AC) to verify that compute_validity returns a DerivedPlanReviewHealth object with the exact specified pin_status enum values, phase_status enum values, enforced boolean, and TargetPinDetail field set.
evidence: 'compute_validity preserves its top-level {valid, reason, verdict} contract and adds health: DerivedPlanReviewHealth'

'That nested contract has pin_status exactly current, current-no-relationships, stale-pin-drift, stale-pin-missing, malformed-pin, or legacy-unpinned; phase_status exactly compatible, incompatible, or malformed; enforced boolean; and targets: list[TargetPinDetail]'

'TargetPinDetail is exactly {canonical_id: string, role: child|prerequisite, pinned_fingerprint: string, current_fingerprint: string|null, pin_status: current|stale-pin-drift|stale-pin-missing|malformed-pin}'
AC 21: 'All detailed review surfaces consume the shared derived-health contract'
findings.finding: The plan defines a detailed DerivedPlanReviewHealth schema (six named pin_status values, three named phase_status values, enforced boolean, and the TargetPinDetail object with five named fields) as a distinct API contract, but AC 21 only verifies that 'all detailed review surfaces consume the shared derived-health contract' — it does not verify the schema's own correctness or completeness, leaving the contract definition itself without an acceptance gate.
findings.location: Settled design > Enforcement and rollout (DerivedPlanReviewHealth and TargetPinDetail definitions); AC 21
scenarios: An implementation emits a health object missing current_fingerprint or using an incorrect pin_status enum value; AC 21 is satisfied because surfaces consume some health object, but the schema contract is violated.
findings.suggested_fix: Revise AC 21 to: 'All detailed review surfaces consume the shared derived-health contract, which exposes pin_status (one of: current, current-no-relationships, stale-pin-drift, stale-pin-missing, malformed-pin, legacy-unpinned), phase_status (compatible, incompatible, or malformed), enforced (boolean), and targets (list of TargetPinDetail with canonical_id, role, pinned_fingerprint, current_fingerprint, and per-target pin_status).'; or split into two ACs.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit documentation callouts (section, doc file, or ADR entry) for: the two new config flags and their interaction, the review-phase and priority-floor manifest fields, and the DerivedPlanReviewHealth schema that is exposed in CLI/MCP/audit/UI.
evidence: "plan-material-pin is documented in advanced attestation/gate documentation, not basic user documentation."
ABSENCE: No corresponding doc callout for review-phase:, priority-floor: manifest lines
ABSENCE: No doc callout for verify.enforce_plan_material_pins or verify.require_plan_review_for_close config keys
ABSENCE: No doc callout for DerivedPlanReviewHealth schema despite it surfacing in detailed CLI show, MCP ticket detail, audit output, close errors, and web UI
findings.finding: The plan calls out documentation placement only for plan-material-pin lines, but does not specify where (or that) the two new user-facing config flags (verify.enforce_plan_material_pins, verify.require_plan_review_for_close), the new manifest fields review-phase and priority-floor, and the DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract (which surfaces in CLI show, MCP, audit, and UI) will be documented for operators and integrators.
findings.location: Health and documentation section; Enforcement and rollout section; Execution reviews and close gate section
scenarios: An operator enabling enforcement for the first time finds no reference documentation for the flag names, their interaction, or the health contract they rely on.
An external tool parsing attestation manifests encounters review-phase or priority-floor lines that are undocumented, leading to incorrect handling.
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Health and documentation' section, add one sentence per artifact: e.g. 'The review-phase and priority-floor manifest fields, both enforcement config flags, and the DerivedPlanReviewHealth schema are documented in the same advanced attestation/gate documentation page as plan-material-pin, with the config flags also referenced in the operator configuration reference.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a measurement plan or spike for the 0.80 execution-phase priority floor: define at least one success metric (e.g., false-block rate on a sample of in-progress-ticket reviews, or comparison against planning-phase threshold distributions) before the value is frozen in protocol.
evidence: 'The 0.80 value is a fixed protocol risk policy for already-started work, not an empirically claimed optimum or project config.'
No fixture, pilot, or success metric is defined to validate 0.80 against real review outcomes or finding severity distributions.
The plan defines a fidelity gate for packed prerequisite evaluation but no analogous validation for the floor threshold.
findings.finding: The 0.80 priority-floor threshold for execution-phase reviews is asserted as fixed policy with no measurement plan, spike, or pilot to validate that it is correctly calibrated — i.e., that it neither causes excessive re-review churn for already-started work nor under-blocks genuinely risky findings.
findings.location: Settled design / Execution reviews and close gate — 'The 0.80 value is a fixed protocol risk policy for already-started work, not an empirically claimed optimum or project config.'
scenarios: 0.80 is too high: routine execution-phase reviews fail on borderline criteria that would not have blocked planning reviews, creating friction for in-progress tickets without safety benefit.
0.80 is too low: genuine execution-phase risks are not surfaced reliably, undermining the protocol risk rationale.
Policy is raised in future (the plan acknowledges this possibility) but no baseline measurement exists to justify or compare the change.
findings.suggested_fix: Run a retrospective analysis on existing review outcomes for tickets that were in_progress at review time: measure what fraction of findings would be newly blocking at 0.80 vs the registry baseline, and confirm the delta is intentional. Document the result as the rationale for 0.80 in the advanced attestation docs or an ADR.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a planned ADR or AGENTS.md section capturing the key novel decisions: (1) computed-on-read attestation validity with signed pins, (2) natural (non-eager) propagation as a correctness invariant with its causal-boundary rationale, and (3) the two-snapshot optimistic locking protocol including why three retries and what 'HEAD equality proves' means for supported writes.
evidence: 'plan-material-pin is documented in advanced attestation/gate documentation, not basic user documentation' — references user-facing docs only, not architectural decision records.
'Health and documentation' section describes only UI/CLI/MCP output surfaces; no ADR or AGENTS.md update is mentioned.

The novel propagation model ('natural rather than eager'), the computed-on-read validity extension, and the two-snapshot locking protocol are described only in this plan ticket, not in a persistent architectural artifact.
findings.finding: The plan introduces several novel architectural decisions — signed plan-material pins extending the DSSE/op-cert record, the natural (non-eager) propagation invariant as a correctness property, and the two-snapshot optimistic locking protocol — but does not include a planned ADR, AGENTS.md section, or persistent design-doc update to capture these decisions for future maintainers.
findings.location: Settled design / Why this approach; Settled design / Signed plan-material pins; Health and documentation
scenarios: A future maintainer modifying the attestation pipeline does not know the propagation model is deliberately non-eager and introduces an eager invalidation event, breaking the design invariant.
The two-snapshot locking protocol is partially reimplemented in a new consumer without the HEAD-equality guard because the rationale was only in a closed ticket.
A rollback or audit scenario requires understanding the signed-pins extension to DSSE records; without a design doc, the team reconstructs reasoning from git history.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a 'Prior art' note to the Settled design explaining why the bespoke `plan-material-pin` manifest line is preferred over using in-toto `resolvedDependencies` (native to the already-used in-toto Statement/DSSE substrate) for recording cross-ticket material dependencies.
evidence: Plan: 'plan-material-pin: <role> <canonical-ticket-id> <material-fingerprint>' — a bespoke manifest line encoding cross-document material dependencies
ADR-0044: 'Signatures cover DSSE (Dead Simple Signing Envelope) Pre-Authentication Encoding bytes' and 'DSSE is the Sigstore / in-toto / SLSA standard'
attest/opcert.py: 'The Statement is serialized to canonical JSON (the DSSE payload)' — rebar already uses in-toto Statements

SLSA provenance spec (slsa.dev) defines `resolvedDependencies: [{uri, digest}]` natively in the in-toto predicate for recording what inputs a build step depended on — the same semantics as plan-material-pin
in-toto ITE-6 link metadata defines `materials: {uri: {hash}}` for exactly this cross-step material binding pattern
ABSENCE: 'Why this approach' does not mention or compare to resolvedDependencies, in-toto link materials, or any SLSA prior art
findings.finding: The plan introduces a bespoke `plan-material-pin` manifest line format to record 'what related-ticket material did this review depend on?' — but this is structurally the same problem solved by SLSA provenance `resolvedDependencies` and in-toto link `materials`, both of which live natively in the in-toto Statement / DSSE substrate rebar already uses. The plan's 'Why this approach' section justifies natural vs. eager propagation (a different axis) but never addresses why the existing in-toto resolvedDependencies mechanism is insufficient or inappropriate. Since rebar already wraps its attestations in in-toto Statements (confirmed in ADR-0044 and attest/opcert.py), this is a concrete prior-art comparison the plan should make before committing to a bespoke line format.
findings.location: Settled design / Signed plan-material pins; Why this approach
scenarios: The bespoke line format adds a new parsing/validation surface (ManifestFormatError cases, duplicate detection, lexicographic ordering) that in-toto resolvedDependencies would provide for free via the existing Statement JSON payload, potentially reducing implementation complexity.
Future interoperability with external SLSA/sigstore tooling that can natively parse resolvedDependencies would require translation from the bespoke format.
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Why this approach' or 'Settled design / Signed plan-material pins' section, add a paragraph: 'SLSA resolvedDependencies / in-toto link materials were considered. They were ruled out because: (1) rebar's manifest is a flat line list, not a JSON predicate — embedding a JSON sub-object for pins would require a new parsing layer; (2) the canonical ticket-ID + role + 16-hex-char fingerprint fits cleanly in the existing manifest grammar; (3) the downstream verifier already authenticates the whole manifest byte-for-byte, so the envelope format adds no security value here.' If this reasoning holds, document it; if it does not, evaluate using resolvedDependencies instead.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `current-no-relationships` to DerivedPlanReviewHealth.pin_status in 75de and verify cb6b's rendering distinguishes it from legacy-unpinned.
evidence: Parent plan Enforcement section: 'pin_status exactly current, current-no-relationships, stale-pin-drift, stale-pin-missing, malformed-pin, or legacy-unpinned'
75de Implementation: 'It has pin_status exactly current, stale-pin-drift, stale-pin-missing, malformed-pin, or legacy-unpinned' — current-no-relationships absent
cb6b AC: 'Clearly distinguish legacy-unpinned from current-no-relationships' — requires the value exist
No other sibling defines DerivedPlanReviewHealth.pin_status
findings.finding: Child 75de defines DerivedPlanReviewHealth.pin_status with exactly five values (current, stale-pin-drift, stale-pin-missing, malformed-pin, legacy-unpinned) but omits the parent-required `current-no-relationships` value, which the parent plan specifies as a distinct status identifying a review that deliberately recorded no related material. Child cb6b's own AC ('Clearly distinguish legacy-unpinned from current-no-relationships') also depends on this value existing in the contract, creating an internal inconsistency between the contract definition (75de) and its consumer (cb6b).
findings.location: child 75de-02b0-d454-410b — DerivedPlanReviewHealth pin_status enum definition; child cb6b-24b4-1056-4ee6 — AC 'Clearly distinguish legacy-unpinned from current-no-relationships'
scenarios: A review on a ticket with no children and no prerequisites signs successfully with an empty pin set; the contract can only classify this as 'legacy-unpinned' (incorrect — legacy-unpinned means no pin lines, not an intentional empty set) or 'current' (incorrect — current means all pins match), leaving the semantically distinct 'deliberate no-relationships' case unrepresentable
cb6b renders advisory drift and attempts to distinguish current-no-relationships from legacy-unpinned but the health contract it consumes never produces that value
findings.suggested_fix: In 75de's DerivedPlanReviewHealth, change the pin_status enum to exactly: current, current-no-relationships, stale-pin-drift, stale-pin-missing, malformed-pin, legacy-unpinned. Define current-no-relationships as the aggregate status when the review completed with zero direct children and zero direct prerequisites (relation_snapshot confirmed empty), yielding an intentionally empty pin set. Update derive_plan_material_pin_health to return current-no-relationships when the input is an empty (not None/absent) sequence produced from a confirmed-empty relation snapshot, reserving legacy-unpinned for absent/None input only.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct 7c27's upstream contract section to attribute PlanRelationSnapshot (with plan text, relation kind, readable state) to acaf-bb4d, not 75de; 75de supplies only PlanMaterialPin, PlanValidityProfile, and compute_validity.
evidence: 7c27 Upstream contract: '75de must deliver the review-time PlanRelationSnapshot preload keyed by authoritative canonical ID with relation kind, readable state, pinned material, and plan text'
acaf Implementation: 'Define PlanRelationSnapshot, PlanMaterialPin, PlanRelationSnapshotError, and tracker_head_sha() in plan_review/relation_snapshot.py'
75de Implementation: 'PlanMaterialPin is child acaf's typed {role, canonical_id, material_fingerprint} record'
75de does not define PlanRelationSnapshot; it only uses PlanMaterialPin from acaf
findings.finding: Child 7c27 declares its upstream contract as requiring 75de to deliver 'the review-time PlanRelationSnapshot preload keyed by authoritative canonical ID with relation kind, readable state, pinned material, and plan text', but PlanRelationSnapshot (including plan text) is defined and owned by child acaf-bb4d, not 75de. 75de itself references PlanRelationSnapshot as 'child acaf's typed record'. If 7c27's implementation-start check verifies 75de for this type (as the text implies with 'verify both symbols and contract tests exist; if either differs, stop as upstream contract drift'), it will incorrectly halt as upstream contract drift even after acaf has landed.
findings.location: child 7c27-10fc-4fe6-4b56 — Upstream contract section: '75de must deliver the review-time PlanRelationSnapshot preload'
scenarios: Developer implementing 7c27 runs the upstream-contract check, looks in 75de's delivered symbols for PlanRelationSnapshot with plan text, finds it absent (because it lives in acaf), and halts the story as upstream contract drift when in fact the correct dependency (acaf) has landed and is complete
7c27 is incorrectly blocked on a 75de deliverable that 75de never intended to own
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '75de must deliver the review-time PlanRelationSnapshot preload keyed by authoritative canonical ID with relation kind, readable state, pinned material, and plan text' with 'acaf must deliver PlanRelationSnapshot (plan_review/relation_snapshot.py) keyed by canonical ID with relation kind, readable state, pinned material, and plan text; 75de must deliver PlanValidityProfile, compute_validity with profile parameter, and DerivedPlanReviewHealth.' Update the implementation-start verification command to check acaf's module for PlanRelationSnapshot and 5967's orchestrator for pass3_over_findings(execution_review=...).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a formal dependency from cb6b on 5967 (for phase_status in DerivedPlanReviewHealth) alongside its existing dependency on 75de.
evidence: cb6b Implementation: 'Consume the validity story's DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract' and 'Display...signed review phase, required phase, and effective execution floor as applicable'
cb6b AC: 'Output schemas, rendering tests, and advanced documentation cover pins, phases, toggles...'
5967 Implementation: 'Extend 75de's DerivedPlanReviewHealth with always-present phase_status: compatible|incompatible|malformed'
cb6b lists no dependency on 5967 in its implementation or acceptance criteria
cb6b only declares an implicit dependency on 75de via 'validity story's DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract'
findings.finding: Child cb6b declares no formal dependency on child 5967 despite consuming DerivedPlanReviewHealth.phase_status, which is only added to the contract by 5967. If cb6b is implemented before 5967 lands, the health contract it consumes will be missing phase_status entirely, causing cb6b's rendering code and AC ('display signed review phase, required phase, and effective execution floor') to target a nonexistent field.
findings.location: child cb6b-24b4-1056-4ee6 — Implementation section and AC referencing phase_status and signed review phase
scenarios: cb6b is claimed and implemented after 75de closes but before 5967 closes; the DerivedPlanReviewHealth available has no phase_status field; cb6b's rendering code and tests fail or are written against a stub
cb6b is implemented concurrently with 5967 and both edit the health contract representation, causing merge conflicts in rendering tests
findings.suggested_fix: In cb6b's implementation or dependencies section, add: 'This story is implemented only after 5967 closes and its phase_status extension to DerivedPlanReviewHealth is merged into the feature branch, in addition to 75de's base health contract.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add formal dependencies from 145e on both 75de (for enforce_plan_material_pins) and 2bbd (for require_plan_review_for_close) to ensure config key registrations precede the dogfood enablement story.
evidence: 145e Implementation: 'Treat the sibling stories as the sole owners of adding each configuration key to the reusable schema and tests/golden/config_surface.json. This story verifies both keys are already present together, enables them only in rebar.toml'
75de owns: 'Register enforce_plan_material_pins: bool = False on _config_schema.py::VerifyConfig'
2bbd owns: 'Register require_plan_review_for_close: bool = False on _config_schema.py::VerifyConfig'
145e lists no formal prerequisites in its plan text
findings.finding: Child 145e declares no formal dependency on children 75de or 2bbd, yet its implementation says it 'verifies both keys are already present together' (enforce_plan_material_pins from 75de and require_plan_review_for_close from 2bbd) and 'does not re-register either golden key'. If 145e is claimed before both 75de and 2bbd have landed and registered their respective config keys, 145e's verification step will fail because the keys it depends on being present do not yet exist in the schema or config-surface golden.
findings.location: child 145e-52a9-26e3-4209 — Implementation section: 'Treat the sibling stories as the sole owners of adding each configuration key'
scenarios: Agent claims 145e after only 75de lands; rebar.toml is edited to set require_plan_review_for_close=true but the key doesn't exist in VerifyConfig yet (2bbd not landed); config load raises ConfigError or silently ignores the key; 145e's lifecycle tests fail on the missing gate
Automated dependency-ordering tools sequence 145e concurrently with 75de/2bbd because no dependency edge exists
findings.suggested_fix: Add to 145e's implementation section: 'This story is implemented only after 75de-02b0 (which registers enforce_plan_material_pins) and 2bbd-87f6 (which registers require_plan_review_for_close) are closed and merged. Both config keys and their config-surface golden entries must be present before enabling them in rebar.toml or running the lifecycle verification suite.'

evidence: Session log child: '9417-a700-297d-4701: Accept runner metadata on focused prerequisite output'

Plan's PrerequisiteCoverageRecord schema: '{prerequisite_id: canonical string, disposition: consistent|finding|indeterminate, findings: list[Finding], reason_code?: evaluation-error|output-invalid|attribution-invalid, detail?: string}' — no runner/model metadata field.
No mention of runner metadata anywhere in the Prerequisite consistency review section or in the manifest/attestation design for per-prerequisite evaluations.
The plan notes 'Multiple prerequisite evaluations may share a call' and describes batching, but does not address how runner/model attribution is recorded per evaluation or per coverage record.
findings.finding: Child ticket 9417 ('Accept runner metadata on focused prerequisite output') is silently absent from the plan's prerequisite output schema and prerequisite evaluation design.
scenarios: An agent implements the prerequisite criterion and produces PrerequisiteCoverageRecords but has no schema slot to carry which model/runner performed each evaluation, making audit traces incomplete for batched calls.
A reviewer attempts to implement 9417 and finds the parent plan's schema contradicts or omits the runner metadata field, requiring an unspecified schema extension.

findings.finding: The plan states the pre-sign transaction operates 'without reducing tickets under the lock,' but the existing implementation's `under_lock_check` inside `generation.sign_manifest` calls `collect()` → `collect_plan_relation_snapshot()` → `reduce_all_tickets()`, performing a full store reduction under the write lock. The plan's wording is ambiguous about whether this is a prescription (the new design should avoid full reduction under lock) or a description (the HEAD-SHA re-check under lock is sufficient). If implementers treat it as prescriptive and move to a HEAD-only under-lock check without a full re-reduction, they must verify that no concurrent write can change relationship membership or fingerprints without advancing HEAD — a non-obvious correctness invariant that depends on every write path appending+committing atomically. The plan should explicitly resolve this ambiguity.
findings.location: Settled design → The phase story: 'Acquire the global lock and require HEAD still equals revision-after, compare the fresh snapshot with the pre-LLM snapshot, then append plus commit the signature under that lock. ... This detects new children or inbound blockers globally without reducing tickets under the lock.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the phase story: 'The under-lock re-check compares the tracker HEAD SHA (not a full store reduction) because every write path appends, stages, and commits atomically — any concurrent relationship membership or fingerprint change necessarily advances HEAD. HEAD equality therefore guarantees generation equality, and the full store reduction occurs outside the lock during the retry loop.'

findings.finding: The plan's enumeration of `pin_status` values omits `current-no-relationships`, which is a live sixth value in the PinStatus type, the ticket_state schema, the MCP model, and the attest module — all of which are named consumer surfaces in the plan.
findings.location: Settled design / Enforcement and rollout: 'pin_status exactly current, stale-pin-drift, stale-pin-missing, malformed-pin, or legacy-unpinned'
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Enforcement and rollout' section, change 'pin_status exactly current, stale-pin-drift, stale-pin-missing, malformed-pin, or legacy-unpinned' to 'pin_status exactly current, current-no-relationships, stale-pin-drift, stale-pin-missing, malformed-pin, or legacy-unpinned; current-no-relationships identifies a current attestation over a ticket that has no direct children or prerequisites at review time.'

findings.finding: The plan specifies that execution reviews apply `effective_block_threshold = max(registry.threshold_for(criterion), 0.80)` to blocking criteria but does not state that `prerequisite-consistency` is explicitly excluded from this max() — the code hard-codes (0.60, True) and bypasses the 0.80 floor for that criterion, which is a decision rule not co-located with the schema.
findings.location: Settled design / Execution reviews and close gate: 'During execution, normal Pass 3 blocking-threshold resolution applies effective_block_threshold = max(registry.threshold_for(criterion), 0.80) to blocking criteria.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence after the max() formula: 'The prerequisite-consistency criterion uses a fixed 0.60 blocking threshold regardless of execution-review phase; it is not subject to the 0.80 floor because prerequisite conflicts are structural incompatibilities evaluated independently of work-in-progress risk.'

findings.finding: The release gate for packed prerequisite evaluation references 'a one-prerequisite-per-call gold baseline' but never specifies how that baseline dataset is constructed — what scenarios it must cover, its minimum size, or where the fixtures live. An executor implementing AC 'Packed prerequisite evaluation matches the one-per-call fidelity contract' cannot know whether their baseline is sufficient to satisfy the gate.
findings.location: Settled design → Prerequisite consistency review (batching paragraph); AC 'Packed prerequisite evaluation matches the one-per-call fidelity contract'
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence such as: 'The gold baseline comprises at least one fixture per coverage disposition (consistent, finding, indeterminate) plus at least one tail-item fixture per bin; fixtures live under tests/fixtures/prereq_packing/.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assert the post-rollback schema state for `plan_review_phase` explicitly: specify whether old-code fresh reductions (non-snapshot path) are permitted to produce snapshots without the field, and if so, add a compatibility test covering old-code reduction from raw history (not just re-compaction of a new-code snapshot) to confirm the bootstrap rule re-applies correctly on next new-code deployment.
evidence: 'Compatibility tests run old reduction plus re-compaction against a phase-bearing snapshot, then roll forward and prove the exact phase value survives' — forward-only, not a backward/fresh-reduction test
'To remove the 0.80 execution-review behavior, first disable both gates, then deploy the preceding code version' — describes process rollback without stating post-rollback schema state
ABSENCE: no assertion that old-code fresh reductions (non-snapshot path) produce a consistent or acceptable schema state for plan_review_phase
findings.finding: The plan asserts that after rollback old reducers 'pass plan_review_phase through unchanged on re-compaction' and deems this rollback-safe, but the compatibility tests described only prove forward survival ('roll forward'). The post-rollback schema STATE is not asserted: if old code performs a fresh reduction from raw history (e.g., after a compaction-cache eviction), it will produce a compiled snapshot without plan_review_phase at all, leaving a mixed persistent state (some snapshots carry the field, fresh reductions under old code do not). The plan's rollback section covers flag/process rollback but does not state what the schema looks like after old-code fresh reductions run alongside snapshots already containing the field.
findings.location: Settled design — Enforcement and rollout: 'Snapshots containing plan_review_phase remain rollback-safe because process_snapshot restores unknown compiled-state keys and compaction drops only its explicit strip set'
scenarios: Old code is redeployed after an incident; a high-traffic ticket's compaction cache is evicted; old reducer rebuilds from raw events and produces a snapshot without plan_review_phase; new code reading that snapshot bootstraps planning unconditionally, incorrectly resetting the phase for an in-progress ticket.

Mixed-version deployment during rollback window: new instances write plan_review_phase into snapshots; old instances performing fresh reductions silently drop it, causing non-deterministic phase values depending on which instance serves a read.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit statement such as: 'After rollback to old code, fresh reductions from raw history will not produce plan_review_phase; this is acceptable because on the next forward deployment the reducer-cache version bump re-bootstraps the field idempotently from compiled status.' Then add a corresponding compatibility test: old-code reduction from raw history -> no field; new-code re-reduction of that snapshot -> field correctly bootstrapped.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
